tasks.description:
Brainfuck interpreter. Given a program (Scott list of
instructions) and an input stream (Scott list of Scott
nats), return the output stream (Scott list of Scott nats).

The tape is infinite in both directions. All cells start
at zero. Cells hold natural numbers; Dec on zero stays
at zero. The loop executes its body while the current
cell is nonzero.

Instruction encoding (7-variant Scott):
  Right = Î»r.Î»l.Î»i.Î»d.Î»o.Î»n.Î»p.r
  Left  = Î»r.Î»l.Î»i.Î»d.Î»o.Î»n.Î»p.l
  Inc   = Î»r.Î»l.Î»i.Î»d.Î»o.Î»n.Î»p.i
  Dec   = Î»r.Î»l.Î»i.Î»d.Î»o.Î»n.Î»p.d
  Out   = Î»r.Î»l.Î»i.Î»d.Î»o.Î»n.Î»p.o
  In    = Î»r.Î»l.Î»i.Î»d.Î»o.Î»n.Î»p.n
  Loop(body) = Î»r.Î»l.Î»i.Î»d.Î»o.Î»n.Î»p.p(body)
where body is itself a Scott list of instructions.

Scott nat: Z = Î»s.Î»z.z, S(n) = Î»s.Î»z.s(n).
Scott list: Nil = Î»c.Î»n.n, Cons(h,t) = Î»c.Î»n.c(h,t).

@main takes (program, input) and returns the output list.

Bresenham's line rasterization. Given two points as
Scott pairs, return a Scott list of all pixel
coordinates on the rasterized line from start to end.

This version only handles cases where the end point
is greater than or equal to the start point in both
coordinates (x1 >= x0 and y1 >= y0).

Scott nat: Z = Î»s.Î»z.z, S(n) = Î»s.Î»z.s(n).
Scott pair: P(x,y) = Î»p.p(x, y).
Scott list: Nil = Î»c.Î»n.n, Cons(h,t) = Î»c.Î»n.c(h,t).

@main(P1, P2) returns the list of points on the line.

tasks.description:
Determine whether a small CNF formula is satisfiable.
Return True (Î»a.Î»b.a) if SAT, False (Î»a.Î»b.b) if UNSAT.

Input: a Scott list of clauses. Each clause is a Scott
list of literals. A literal is either:
  Pos(n) = Î»p.Î»n.p(n)  â€” variable n is positive
  Neg(n) = Î»p.Î»n.n(n)  â€” variable n is negated
where n is a Scott nat giving the variable index.

An empty formula (no clauses) is satisfiable. A formula
with an empty clause is unsatisfiable.

Scott nat: Z = Î»s.Î»z.z, S(n) = Î»s.Î»z.s(n).
Scott list: Nil = Î»c.Î»n.n, Cons(h,t) = Î»c.Î»n.c(h,t).
Church bool: True = Î»a.Î»b.a, False = Î»a.Î»b.b.

Lambda calculus evaluator. Given a Scott-encoded lambda
term using de Bruijn indices, return its beta-normal form
(also Scott-encoded).

Term encoding (3-variant Scott):
  Lam(body)    = Î»lam.Î»app.Î»var.lam(body)
  App(fun,arg) = Î»lam.Î»app.Î»var.app(fun, arg)
  Var(idx)     = Î»lam.Î»app.Î»var.var(idx)
where idx is a Scott nat (de Bruijn index).

Scott nat: Z = Î»s.Î»z.z, S(n) = Î»s.Î»z.s(n).

De Bruijn convention: Var(0) inside one Lam refers to
that binder; higher indices reach outer binders or are
free. Beta-reduction must handle shifting and
substitution of de Bruijn indices correctly.

@main takes one Term and returns its beta-normal form.

Convex hull of 2D integer points.

Input: a Scott list of points. Each point is a Scott
pair Î»p.p(x, y) where x, y are Scott nats.

Output: a Scott list of points on the convex hull in
counter-clockwise order, starting from the
lexicographically smallest point (smallest x first,
then smallest y). Collinear boundary points are
excluded â€” only true vertices of the hull.

Degenerate cases: for 1 point return it; for 2 points
return both in lex order; for all-collinear input
return the two endpoints.

Scott nat: Z = Î»s.Î»z.z, S(n) = Î»s.Î»z.s(n)
Scott list: Nil = Î»c.Î»n.n, Cons(h,t) = Î»c.Î»n.c(h,t)
Scott pair: P(x,y) = Î»p.p(x, y)

Shortest path in a 2D grid. The input is a grid
encoded as a Scott list of rows, where each row is
a Scott list of Church booleans: True means open,
False means wall. Find the shortest path from the
top-left corner (0,0) to the bottom-right corner
(H-1,W-1), moving up/down/left/right through open
cells only. Return the path length as a Scott nat
(number of steps). If no path exists, return Z (0).
Start and end cells are guaranteed open.

Scott nat: Z = Î»s.Î»z.z, S(n) = Î»s.Î»z.s(n).
Scott list: Nil = Î»c.Î»n.n, Cons(h,t) = Î»c.Î»n.c(h,t).
Church bool: True = Î»a.Î»b.a, False = Î»a.Î»b.b.

Given a connected undirected weighted graph, compute the
total weight of a minimum spanning tree. Return the sum
of edge weights in the MST as a Scott nat.

Input: a Scott nat N (node count) and a Scott list of
edges. Each edge is a triple Î»e.e(U, V, W) where U and
V are Scott nat node indices and W is the Scott nat
weight.

Scott list: Nil = Î»c.Î»n.n, Cons(h,t) = Î»c.Î»n.c(h,t).
Scott triple: T(a,b,c) = Î»t.t(a, b, c).

tasks.description:
Type-check a simply-typed lambda calculus term. Given a
Scott-encoded STLC term with type annotations, return a
Church boolean: True if the term is well-typed in the
empty context, False otherwise.

Types use de Bruijn indices for base types:
  Base(n)  = Î»base.Î»arr.base(n)
  Arr(a,b) = Î»base.Î»arr.arr(a, b)

Terms use de Bruijn indices for variables:
  Lam(ty, body) = Î»lam.Î»app.Î»var.lam(ty, body)
  App(fun, arg) = Î»lam.Î»app.Î»var.app(fun, arg)
  Var(idx)      = Î»lam.Î»app.Î»var.var(idx)

where n and idx are Scott nats:
  Z = Î»s.Î»z.z, S(n) = Î»s.Î»z.s(n).

Church booleans: True = Î»a.Î»b.a, False = Î»a.Î»b.b.

Two types are equal iff they have identical structure.
In App(f, x), f must have type Arr(A, B) and x must
have type A; the result has type B.

list of 4 Scott nats. Empty cells are Z (zero),
filled cells use S(Z) through S(S(S(S(Z)))) for
digits 1 through 4.

Output: the completed grid in the same format with
every cell filled (1-4), satisfying:
- Each row contains 1, 2, 3, 4 exactly once
- Each column contains 1, 2, 3, 4 exactly once
- Each 2Ã—2 box contains 1, 2, 3, 4 exactly once

The puzzle always has a unique solution.

Compute the Discrete Fourier Transform
using the GN number system.

Balanced Ternary (BT), Scott-encoded, LSB first:
  E       = Î»t.Î»o.Î»i.Î»e.e
  T(rest) = Î»t.Î»o.Î»i.Î»e.t(rest)   (digit -1)
  O(rest) = Î»t.Î»o.Î»i.Î»e.o(rest)   (digit  0)
  I(rest) = Î»t.Î»o.Î»i.Î»e.i(rest)   (digit +1)
Example: T(I(E)) = -1 + 1*3 = 2.
Outputs must be canonical (no trailing O).

GN numbers are Scott trees of BT integers:
  L(n)   = Î»n.Î»l.l(n)     (scalar)
  B(a,b) = Î»n.Î»l.n(a,b)   (pair: a + b*w)

For N-point FFT (N = 2^k), each coefficient is a
GN(k-1) number. Input coefficients are in
bit-reversal order:
  [c0,c1,c2,c3] is stored as B(B(c0,c2),B(c1,c3))
The DFT evaluates c0 + c1*x + c2*xÂ² + ...
